body:
There seems to be some kind of rebinding loop - triggered by the fact that tailnet knows it doesn't have a usable UDP socket.

```
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.982 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: last netcheck reported send error. Rebinding."
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.982 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: [warning] failed to force-set UDP read buffer size to 7340032: operation not permitted; using kernel default values (impacts throughput only)"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.982 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: [warning] failed to force-set UDP write buffer size to 7340032: operation not permitted; using kernel default values (impacts throughput only)"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.982 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: [warning] failed to force-set UDP read buffer size to 7340032: operation not permitted; using kernel default values (impacts throughput only)"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: [warning] failed to force-set UDP write buffer size to 7340032: operation not permitted; using kernel default values (impacts throughput only)"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: Rebind; defIf=\"eth0\", ips=[192.168.100.148/24 fe80::5054:ff:fee8:4244/64]"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: closing connection to derp-999 (rebind-default-route-change), age 1s"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: 0 active derp conns"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.984 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: adding connection to derp-999 for home-keep-alive"
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.984 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: 1 active derp conns: derp-999=cr0s,wr0s"
```

But also, it only triggers reconnecting to DERP if the DERP connection local address is not one of the interface addresses.  I can't help but wonder whether in tests we connect over the loopback or something.
The other annoying thing about this test is that it runs two commands over PTYs, one for the agent, one for the ping client, so their output is jumbled together in the logs.

Ok, I think I at least understand the problem(s).

First up:

```
2023-09-18 11:06:44.982 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: last netcheck reported send error. Rebinding.
```

This happens because netcheck is supposed to verify that we can send a UDP packet to at least one STUN server.  Since we disable all STUN servers when we disable P2P, netcheck always fails this test. Tailscale thinks that we can't send any IPv4 packets and should rebind our sockets, I guess because the usual reason you can't send UDP packets is that you're bound to the wrong interface (e.g. Wifi getting turned on & off, VM getting network interfaces connected and disconnected).

Next up,

```
2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: Rebind; defIf=\"eth0\", ips=[192.168.100.148/24 fe80::5054:ff:fee8:4244/64]
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: closing connection to derp-999 (rebind-default-route-change), age 1s
"2023-09-18 11:06:44.983 [debu]  net.tailnet.net.wgengine: magicsock: 0 active derp conns
```

While rebinding, tailscale checks whether the DERP connections local address match the address(es) it discovered for the preferred interface.  In most sane real systems, the outgoing DERP connection should use an outgoing interface.  But, in our tests, DERP uses `localhost` and so tailscale tears down the DERP connection and recreates it.

The net result of all of this is that tailscale is constantly tearing down and recreating the DERP connections on both sides, so sometimes the connections never stay up long enough for our ping to get through.

I think that, fundamentally, our method of disabling P2P is breaking Tailscale assumptions.  Namely: Tailscale assumes that we can send UDP to at least one STUN server, and if we can't then we are likely broken at the IP socket layer and need to rebind.

Furthermore, our method of disabling P2P leaves the binding to UDP socket in place, meaning that workspaces have an open socket.  If you could somehow figure out the IP address of a workspace, you could send it Disco packets and it would happily set up a tailscale connection with you.  This all assumes you have the right crypto keys, which means you have to be an authorized user of the workspace and still go through the coordinator, but when customers ask us to disable P2P for security or compliance reasons, they probably won't be happy that we still have this open socket you can get in through.

@deansheather @coadler I'd like your thoughts on the following

Option 1
=======

A relatively limited tailscale fix would do something like spin through the DERP map, and if _every_ region has STUN disabled, then lie in the netcheck and say UDP is fine.  With no STUN servers at all, rebinding is just never going to do us any good.

Option 2
=======

A more extensive tailscale fix would involve us adding a configuration option to directly tell tailscale not to bother with UDP binding & STUN at all.  We never open a UDP listening socket, and disable all STUN interactions.
tailscale PR: https://github.com/coder/tailscale/pull/40 equivalent to Option 1 above
Tailscale PR was merged and included in https://github.com/coder/coder/pull/9817
